brief: GAP/RATIONALE: In symptomatic Brugada syndrome (BrS), how the size/extent of the arrhythmogenic substrate predicts prognosis is unclear; long-term safety+efficacy of epicardial RFA vs ICD-only not validated. Aim: assess prognostic impact of substrate size and validate long-term outcomes of epicardial radiofrequency ablation (RFA) vs no-RFA. DESIGN/SETTING: prospective investigational long-term registry study. POPULATION: 257 selected symptomatic BrS patients, all with ICD implantation. GROUPS: 206 underwent epicardial RFA, monitored >5 yr post-ablation (RFA group); 51 declined RFA, received ICD only (no-RFA group). Substrates localized on epicardial surface of right ventricle. Ajmaline used to guide epicardial mapping/ablation. ENDPOINTS: (1) risk factors for VF events pre-ablation; (2) freedom from VF events post-ablation. RESULTS pre-RFA follow-up (median 27 months): 53 patients had VF episodes/VF storms. Independent risk factors: substrate size HR 1.13 (95% CI 1.08-1.18, P<0.001); aborted cardiac arrest HR 2.98 (95% CI 1.68-5.28, P<0.001); SCN5A variants HR 2.22 (95% CI 1.15-4.27, P=0.017). RESULTS post-RFA follow-up (median 40 months): RFA group had superior outcomes vs no-RFA (P<0.001), with no major procedure-related complications. TAKEAWAY: BrS substrate extent is a crucial prognostic factor for recurrent VF; epicardial RFA is safe and effective vs no-RFA; ajmaline-guided epicardial mapping/ablation is valuable; supports future exploration of non-invasive methods to guide decisions.
